So I think it started, so I started my career early on at IBM Security, and I think one of the first things I realized was marketing tended to not be compounding.
It was always diminishing, and it was always hard to get attention, unless you're just dumping money in ads.

And I think at the time, IBM was spending maybe like a billion a year in marketing, and my thought process was always, why, like if you took half of that money, you could literally buy every publication and every newsletter and every category you care about.
So why wouldn't you do that?
You're hedging one year of marketing budget.
Like that seemed like an endeavor that was worthwhile, you know, but people never did it.
People just never thought like as publishers, you know, and so for me, I wanted to kind of prove that model.
That's just normal human principle.
I always love psychologists.
So it's like, how do you build trust with people?
Like you deliver value and, you know, and by being really useful and you just create an engine that does that and they want to do business with you.
So I built the site called secureintelligence.com.
It was the first content hub outside of ibm.com and it actually like started a lot of the process of ibm.com reinventing itself.
A good friend of mine now owns all of ibm.com and he was the person that I built it with and it actually lasted 15 years.

just folded it back last year, but it was just proving that if you just do content really well in the security space, for example, we were just having our research team doing zero vulnerabilities and publishing that content and it just grew.
So the first year we did about $17 million in revenue directly from traffic on that site.
And so from there, I just built my whole career on that premise of every job I've ever had.
I helped that company become more like a publisher.
And try to get the leaders to be more like creators rather than, you know, and build influence and build audience.

built this community called AI Exchange.

Developing these assignments, you need to create several variants of every single possible query that people might come up with in order to find that content.
